paper_title: Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Many everyday programming tasks resist clean rule-based implementation, such as alerting on important log lines, repairing malformed JSON, or ranking search results by intent, and are increasingly outsourced to large language model APIs at the cost of locality, reproducibility, and price. We propose fuzzy-function programming: compiling such a function from a natural-language specification into a compact, locally-executable neural artifact. We instantiate this paradigm with Program-as-Weights (PAW), in which a 4B compiler trained on FuzzyBench, a 10M-example dataset we release, emits parameter-efficient adapters for a frozen, lightweight interpreter. A 0.6B Qwen3 interpreter executing PAW programs matches the performance of direct prompting of Qwen3-32B, while using roughly one fiftieth of the inference memory and running at 30 tokens/s on a MacBook M3. PAW reframes the foundation model from a per-input problem solver into a tool builder: invoked once per function definition, it produces a small reusable artifact whose subsequent calls per function application are cheap and offline.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Many everyday programming tasks resist clean rule-based implementation, such as alerting on important log lines, repairing malformed JSON, or ranking search results by intent, and are increasingly outsourced to large language model APIs at the cost of locality, reproducibility, and price. We propose fuzzy-function programming: compiling such function from natural-language specification into compact, locally-executable neural artifact. We instantiate this paradigm with Program-asWeights (PAW), in which 4B compiler trained on FuzzyBench, 10M-example dataset we release, emits parameter-efficient adapters for frozen, lightweight interpreter. 0.6B Qwen3 interpreter executing PAW programs matches the performance of direct prompting of Qwen3-32B, while using roughly one fiftieth of the inference memory and running at 30 tokens/s on MacBook M3. PAW reframes the foundation model from per-input problem solver into tool builder: invoked once per function definition, it produces small reusable artifact whose subsequent calls per function application are cheap and offline.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Programming has historically been about writing explicit rules in formal language designed for the purpose, programming language. function is defined by code, and the computer executes it deterministically. For many tasks, this paradigm works beautifully: sorting numbers, processing structured data, computing matrix products. Yet large class of real-world functions resists precise specification. Consider, for instance, filtering computer log to alert someone only on the log lines or messages that matter, repairing malformed JSON, or ranking search results by intent. Even apparently simple tasks, such as writing regular expression to parse text with many edge cases, prove brittle. Beyond underspecification, real-world inputs are noisy: typos and format drift routinely break hand-written rules and regexes. These are fuzzy functions [Rubio Manzano, 2012]: problems that humans find intuitive but that cannot be fully captured by crisp symbolic rules. Today, developers frequently outsource such fuzziness to LLM APIs. It is increasingly common to find codebases where remote LLM is called (e.g., gpt(extract answer, text)) to implement functions that are otherwise intractable to program. This approach is undeniably convenient, but it is costly, fragile, undermines reproducibility because providers may silently update their models [Kim et al., 2023], and prevents software from being self-contained. We propose different paradigm with three steps: the developer describes the function in natural language; neural compiler turns that description into small neural binary; and frozen, lightweight Preprint. Figure 1: Overview of the Program-as-Weights paradigm. Top: compile once in the cloud. natural-language description of fuzzy function (here, classify if this is urgent) is fed to neural compiler, which produces neural program. Bottom: run locally. small frozen neural interpreter loads the compiled program and runs the users input (Need your signature by EOD!) to produce the output (urgent). The compiled program is single file that can be cached, version-controlled, and called offline like any other library function. neural interpreter, installed once on the users device, runs that binary just like user-defined function (Figure 1). We call this paradigm Program-as-Weights (PAW). Any sufficiently expressive parameterefficient module (PEFT) emitted by hypernetwork can serve as the program form; we instantiate two, prefix-tuning and text-to-LoRA, and find LoRA better, with future PEFTs possibly better still. PAW program has two halves. The first is pseudo-program in natural language, restatement of the users specification. The second is PEFT module that re-tunes the frozen interpreter for this one task: in our precursor system this was prefix-tuning KV cache; in our current system it is LoRA generated by the compiler from its own hidden states and injected into the interpreter. The discrete half shields the interpreter from typos and ambiguity in the original specification; the continuous half supplies the fine-grained behavioral control that text alone cannot. The compile pipeline has two stages, both running 4B Qwen3 models. The first stage is pseudo compiler, an off-the-shelf model we never train: prompted with small task-rewriting template, it turns the users spec into clean pseudo-program of paraphrased description plus handful of input-output examples. The second stage is LoRA compiler that we train: it reads the spec and the pseudo-program and emits the LoRA. We train the LoRA compiler on FUZZYBENCH, 10M-example dataset we release with this paper, built incrementally across 29 thematic versions covering more than 800 categories of fuzzy text tasks such as classification, format conversion, parsing, fuzzy matching, natural-language commands, agentic tool use, and many more. The result is small, fast, and accurate system. Qwen3-0.6B interpreter executing PAW programs outperforms direct prompting of Qwen3-32B (73.78% vs. 68.70% exact match) at roughly one fiftieth the inference memory. Quantized, the same system runs at 30 tokens per second on MacBook M3 from 430 MB GGUF base shared across functions plus 23 MB per-program LoRA adapter; smaller GPT-2 path runs entirely client-side in the browser via WebAssembly. We see Program-as-Weights as concrete step toward small-model future [Belcak et al., 2025], in which the heavy lifting happens once at compile time and the day-to-day work of running software happens locally. We illustrate its applications in five case studies: output triage (event-driven log monitoring), custom classification (intent-based site navigation), fuzzy search (semantic search reranking), agent preprocessing (a tool-calling pipeline that scores 93% on TOOLCALL-15), and creative generation (a multilingual word-guessing game). Each is the kind of fuzzy task that resists symbolic implementation but does not need an API call to 30B-parameter model on every input. We additionally show the abstractions modality generality: replacing only the compiler with vision-language model while keeping the same interpreter runs PAW programs on image-conditioned fuzzy tasks. Our code can be found at https://github.com/programasweights and public demo is available at https://programasweights.com.

sections.title: 2 Programs as Weights
sections.content: Let : denote function whose behavior is more naturally specified through natural language, examples, or constraints than through symbolic code, fuzzy function. Instead of repeatedly invoking an LLM to approximate , we propose to compile neural program that specializes fixed model to implement . Formally, let denote user specification, expressed in natural language and optionally accompanied by example input-output pairs (x, y). neural Compiler maps to program p. small fixed neural Interpreter executes on inputs to produce outputs ˆy : = Compiler(s), ˆy = Interpreter(p, x) (x). (1) This division mirrors classical programming, where compiler translates source code into an executable that is later run by runtime. The crucial difference is that the executable here is learned parameter blob, and the runtime is neural network. The interpreter does not need to be retrained: introducing new fuzzy function only requires compiling new program p. Hybrid programs. For conceptual simplicity, may be viewed as single continuous object. In our concrete instantiation, however, is hybrid of discrete and continuous component: = (cid:0)pdiscrete, pcontinuous The discrete component pdiscrete is variable-length sequence of tokens that acts as self-contained pseudo-program presented to the interpreter as part of its input. The continuous component pcontinuous can be implemented using any PEFT method, such as LoRA injected into the interpreter. (cid:1). (2) Why program? This framing matters because it determines how the artifact is used. compiled PAW program is single file (23 MB at Q4_0 for 0.6B interpreter, plus one-time shared base) that can be saved, version-controlled, distributed via package managers, and called from Python or JavaScript with two-line API. PAW programs are objects of the same kind as Python modules: they have name and version, but their behavior is encoded in weights rather than in source code. The compiler is the part that does the heavy lifting; the interpreter is fixed runtime, comparable to CPU or byte-code interpreter in conventional software stacks.

sections.title: 3 The Compiler–Interpreter System
sections.content: 3.1 Compilerinterpreter abstraction The PAW pipeline has three components, none of which depend on the specific PEFT chosen for the program form. pseudo compiler Cp reads the spec and produces discrete pseudo-program pdiscrete. PEFT compiler CPEFT reads the spec together with pdiscrete and emits small parameterefficient module pcontinuous from its hidden states. The frozen interpreter ingests pcontinuous at runtime by attaching it to the appropriate target modules and running the users input through it to produce the output ˆy. We instantiate the PEFT module in two ways: prefix-tuning KV cache (Section 3.3) and LoRA (Section 3.2), with the latter being our current best. Pseudo compiler. The pseudo compiler Cp is an off-the-shelf Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 model that we never train. Given specification s, we prompt Cp with small task-rewriting template (full text in Appendix C) that asks for clean restatement of the task plus handful of representative input-output examples. The output is the discrete component pdiscrete of the program.1 The pseudo compiler is shared by both PEFT instantiations below. 3.2 Text-to-LoRA: our current best LoRA compiler. The LoRA compiler CL is second 4B Qwen3 model, initialized from the same checkpoint as Cp but trained (Section 4). Given the spec and the pseudo-program pdiscrete produced 1In an early prototype we trained single compiler to generate this discrete component via reinforcement learning, but observed that, regardless of seed prompt, the compiler converged on this same paraphrase-plus-examples format; we therefore eliminate the RL stage by directly hand-crafting prompt that produces this format from an off-the-shelf model. 3 Figure 2: Text-to-LoRA instantiation of PAW (Section 3.2). Left. The trained LoRA compiler reads the function specification, the pseudo-program produced by an off-the-shelf prompted pseudo compiler Cp (not depicted), and fixed sequence of learned prefix tokens; it emits prefix-position hidden states H. Middle. The LoRA mapper mean-pools H, passes it through an MLP, and projects into mixing coefficients that compose LoRA matrices (Aex, Bex) over shared learnable bases (eq. (3)). Right. The frozen interpreter ingests pdiscrete prepended to the user input x, with the LoRA hotattached, and generates the output autoregressively. The same pipeline holds for the prefix-tuning precursor (Section 3.3, with architecture in Figure 18); only the mapping from compiler hidden states to PEFT module changes (LoRA KV-cache mapper). by Cp, CL runs single forward pass on the concatenation [s pdiscrete EOS τ1, . . . , τT ], where τ1:T is fixed sequence of = 64 learned prefix tokens. We extract prefix-position hidden states from compiler layers spaced uniformly by depth ratio (one per interpreter layer), and stack them into the tensor RLT dteacher that is fed to the LoRA mapper (Figure 2). LoRA mapper. The LoRA compilers hidden states are converted into per-example LoRA weights by small parameter-efficient module, the LoRA mapper. For each interpreter target-module type (attention q/k/v/o and MLP gate/up/down), the mapper maintains shared learnable bases 1:N RN rd(m) A(m) in , 1:N RN d(m) B(m) out r. (cid:80) These hidden states are mean-pooled over both the depth-aligned layers and the prefix positions, = 1 l,t Hl,t, passed through shallow MLP trunk ϕ, and projected into mixing coefficients LT αA,B l,m,n for each layer l, module type m, and basis index n, via single linear head. The LoRA at layer and module is Aex l,m = (cid:88) n=1 l,m,n A(m) αA , Bex l,m = (cid:88) n=1 l,m,n B(m) αB . (3) We use rank = 64 and = 64 shared bases per module type, applied to all layers and module types. Per fuzzy function, this injects approximately 38.5M LoRA parameters into the interpreter.2 Interpreter. The interpreter is frozen language model. To execute PAW program on input x, we (i) attach the LoRA in eq. (3) to the appropriate target modules, (ii) prepend pdiscrete to the input x, and (iii) generate the output autoregressively. Because the interpreter is frozen and the LoRA hot-swappable, single device-resident interpreter can serve unboundedly many PAW programs; Figure 19 illustrates this one runtime, many programs picture for three example specifications. 3.3 Prefix-tuning: precursor instantiation Prefix compiler. Our precursor system replaced the LoRA mapper with prefix-tuning mapper. The prefix compiler CP is second 4B Qwen3 model trained the same way as CL, with the only difference being how its prefix-position hidden states are consumed. Given [s pdiscrete EOS τ1:T ], CP produces hidden states RLT dteacher at the same depth-aligned layers as in Section 3.2. Instead of pooling and projecting into LoRA weights, small linear mapper ψ projects these hidden l,t ) R2dint that are prepended to the interpreters states position-wise into KV pairs (K ex l,t, ex 2We compare this design to several more-expressive alternatives (per-position aggregation, per-layer bases, per-position with per-layer bases, LoRA with prefix-tuning) in Section 7; none improve over the simple shared-basis design. attention KV cache at every layer, in the manner of standard prefix-tuning [Li and Liang, 2021] (see Figure 18 in the appendix for an architecture diagram). The interpreter then runs through its frozen attention with the additional prefix-position keys and values visible to every query. Both methods solve the task. At controlled comparison scale (same amount of training compute), the prefix-tuning instantiation reaches 50.4% exact match on FuzzyBench, while the LoRA instantiation reaches 56.5% at r=18 (r=18 matches the prefix-tuning program size) and 65.7% at r=64  (Table 1)  . Both outperform the no-compiler prompting baseline (9.8%). LoRA is the stronger PEFT and is the instantiation we scale to the full training data in subsequent experiments. sections.title: 4 Training
sections.content: Only the PEFT compiler is trained. The pseudo compiler Cp is held off-the-shelf and frozen; the interpreter is also frozen. The PEFT compiler is trained to produce PEFT adapter that, when injected into the frozen interpreter alongside fixed pseudo-program, maximizes the likelihood of the target output. With both endpoints frozen, this reduces to single supervised objective. We concentrate below on the LoRA instantiation; the prefix-tuning precursor (Section 3.3) was trained with the same SFT recipe described below, substituting the prefix-tuning mapper for the LoRA mapper. Objective. For each training triple (s, x, y), we look up pre-generated pseudo-program pdiscrete = Cp(s), run forward pass through CL on [s pdiscrete EOS τ1:T ] to obtain prefix-position hidden states, pass those through the LoRA mapper to obtain pLoRA, and inject the result into the interpreter. The loss is the negative mean-token log-likelihood of the target under the frozen interpreter: (cid:0)y (cid:12) (cid:12) pdiscrete, pLoRA(θ; s, pdiscrete), x(cid:1)(cid:3) , L(θ) = E(s,x,y) (cid:2) log Pϕ (4) where θ is the parameters of CL and the LoRA mapper, and ϕ are the interpreter parameters. The gradient flows back through the frozen interpreter into the LoRA mapper and from there into CLs hidden states. Full hyperparameters and compute setup are in Appendix G.

sections.title: 5 FuzzyBench: A 10M-Example Dataset of Fuzzy Functions
sections.content: A central obstacle to training PAW-style methods is the lack of public dataset for compile fuzzy function from specification. We construct FuzzyBench, 10M-example dataset in which every example is triple (s, x, y) of (specification, input, target output), generated using gpt-5.2. Construction. We use two-stage pipeline. In the first stage, we prompt gpt-5.2 to generate natural-language specifications of fuzzy functions. Each prompting call produces eight specifications, and we run repeated calls under different category constraints to cover the breadth of fuzzy tasks developers actually encounter. In the second stage, for each specification, we prompt gpt-5.2 again to generate eight input/output pairs. Specifications are split 80/10/10 by spec into train/validation/test, so that test specifications are entirely unseen at training time. For evaluation, we construct verified test set on which an independent strong model (gpt-5-mini) and gpt-5.2 agree on the output, removing examples where the target itself is ambiguous. Full prompts are in Appendix B. Thematic coverage. FuzzyBench is built incrementally over 29 versions, each adding 100K-500K examples covering new family of fuzzy tasks. Figure 3 groups the resulting 10M examples into seven high-level task families that span what developers actually encounter in fuzzy logic: from raw text processing and parsing to agentic tool use, web intelligence, code-and-command generation, and 5 Figure 3: FuzzyBench-10M task-family distribution. 29 incremental thematic versions are mapped to 7 high-level families. Core text processing & NLP is the largest family because the v1 base layer (2.5M examples; 277 base categories) covers parsing, classification, NER, coreference, and sentiment; the remaining 7.5M examples spread across the other six families. safety/verification. The full per-version timeline (29 entries; the first version alone establishes 277 base categories, and the final dataset covers more than 800 sub-categories) is in Appendix F. Noise variants. For robustness evaluation, we additionally release noise-perturbed versions of the test set along eight axes: typos, grammar errors, ambiguity, formatting drift, all noise (combined), terse phrasing, casual phrasing, and paraphrase. Each axis comes in three intensity levels (light, medium, heavy). Section 8 reports robustness numbers. Empirical ceiling. The data-generating model itself, gpt-5.2, achieves 96.09%; gpt-5-mini achieves 91.87%. These bound how high any compiled function trained on this data can reach.

sections.title: 6 Main Results
sections.content: We compare PAW against three families of baselines, all evaluated on the same test sets as PAW so that any compute or data-generation differences are absorbed in the comparison. (i) Direct prompting of open-weight LMs (Qwen3 0.6B, 4B, 8B, 14B, 32B; OLMo3-7B; Baselines. gpt-oss-20B), and of two API models that bound the empirical ceiling (gpt-5-mini and gpt-5.2). (ii) Symbolic code generation: ALCHEmists LM-to-code pipeline [Huang et al., 2024b], where strong LM writes Python code to solve the fuzzy task and the code is executed at inference. (iii) Standard adaptation of the same 0.6B base: full fine-tuning across 1-4 epochs, and fixed (noncompiler-generated) LoRAs at ranks {18, 64, 128}. Main result. Table 2 summarizes the main numbers. 0.6B-parameter interpreter executing PAW programs achieves 73.78% exact match on FuzzyBench, outperforming prompting Qwen3-32B (68.70%) while using approximately 50 less inference memory (1.2 GB at bf16 vs. 60 GB). Cross-interpreter scaling. Among three interpreters GPT-2 124M, Qwen3 0.6B, and Qwen3.5 0.8B, Qwen3 0.6B is the strongest interpreter; the hybrid 0.8B is slightly weaker. GPT-2 124M, despite having only 1/5 the parameters of Qwen3 0.6B and no instruction tuning, still achieves 54%, suggesting that the compiler-generated LoRA can encode usable task adaptations even into very small, weakly-capable bases. Multimodal generalization without changing the interpreter. The compiler-interpreter abstraction extends to image-conditioned fuzzy functions without changing the interpreter. We swap the text-only Qwen3-4B-Instruct compiler for the same-family Qwen3-VL-4B compiler [Bai et al., 2025], keep the same Qwen3 0.6B interpreter, and reuse the same LoRA mapper. sections.title: 7 Ablations
sections.content: Architectural variants of the LoRA mapper. We tried several variants of the LoRA mapper that, on paper, are strictly more expressive than the default (mean-pool over prefix tokens, shallow trunk, shared bases). Each made things worse. Table 4 reports accuracy across these variants. The simplest design of mean-pooling the prefix-token hidden states into one vector, running single residual MLP, and projecting to mixing coefficients over shared basis set, is the strongest. We do not have clean theoretical explanation for this; we report the finding so that future work need not rediscover it. Compiler vs. no compiler. Table 5 compares PAW with three same-base no compiler baselines on FuzzyBench: full fine-tuning of the 0.6B Qwen3 interpreter, and per-task fixed LoRAs at three ranks. The same data, the same base model, the same training budget; only the compiler is removed. PAW exceeds full fine-tuning by 15.4 percentage points and the strongest fixed LoRA by 21.7 points, showing that the gain comes specifically from compiler-generated LoRA. 7 Table 4: Architectural variants of the LoRA mapper. sections.title: 8 Robustness to Noisy Specifications
sections.content: Real specifications written by developers are noisy: they contain typos, ambiguity, and grammar errors. We evaluate PAW on noise-perturbed versions of the test_clean specifications across seven axes (typos, grammar, ambiguity, formatting, all-noise combined, terse, paraphrase). Table 6 reports the robustness results. PAW degrades only slightly under heavy noise but why? We hypothesise that this robustness is mediated by the discrete pseudo-program: the 4B compiler converts the noisy spec into clean restatement before the small interpreter ever sees it. To test the hypothesis, we trained variant that bypasses the pseudo-program and feeds the raw spec directly to the interpreter. The result  (Table 7)  confirms the hypothesis. On clean inputs, feeding the pseudo-program rather than the raw spec is only 1.6 points better. On heavy-typo specifications, however, the gap widens to 4.5 points. The compiler, 4B LM whose entire job is to read fuzzy specifications and emit clean restatement, effectively denoises the input that the small interpreter sees, which is why PAW degrades little when the original specification is corrupted.

sections.title: 9 Local Execution
sections.content: Beyond benchmarks, to make PAW practical to use, we built developer interface. Developer interface. PAW program is single file that can be downloaded, cached, and called via small Python or JavaScript API. Figure 4 shows complete minimal Python example: paw.compile(prompt) sends specification to compiler service and returns serializable program object; paw.function(id_or_path) loads compiled program and exposes it as Python callable. After the first download, all execution happens locally with no external API calls. Quantization without measurable accuracy loss. On-device execution requires small footprint. We quantize both the shared interpreter base and each per-program LoRA adapter to GGUF formats compatible with llama.cpp [Gerganov and llama.cpp contributors, 2023]. Table 8 reports our 8 import programasweights as paw import programasweights as paw # (1) Describe the fuzzy function spec = """ Classify if this email requires immediate attention . Event-driven log monitoring replaces the naive wait-based terminal watching in Cursor with local classifier that fires only on the lines that matter. Intent-based site navigation provides natural-language quick-find for website without an LLM API call per request. Semantic search reranking adds intent-aware fuzzy search to an existing keyword index, again without putting an LLM in the request path. For tool calling, 10-PAW-function pipeline scores 93% on TOOLCALL-15, capturing tool-routing behavior usually reserved for much larger models. The multilingual word-guessing game (Alien-Taboo) is fuzzy interactive game in which each player turn is served by 0.6B PAW interpreter on small server, with one PAW program per language; the LLM is invoked only at compile time, which is what makes game of this kind economical to host. Full details are in Appendix M.

sections.title: 10 Related Work
sections.content: Hypernetworks. Hypernetworks [Ha et al., 2017] generate the weights of target network from small embedding, originally for vision and language modeling; subsequent work used them for continual learning [von Oswald et al., 2020], multi-task NLP via shared hypernetworks across tasks and layers [Karimi Mahabadi et al., 2021b], and as parameter-efficient adapters in their own right [Karimi Mahabadi et al., 2021a]. The text-conditioned subfamily relevant to PAW maps natural-language task description to PEFT parameters in single forward pass: Hypter [Ye and Ren, 2021] generates BART-Large adapters from descriptions; HINT [Ivison et al., 2023] generates prefixand-adapter modules from instructions to amortize per-query encoding cost; HyperTuning [Phang et al., 2023] introduces T5-based hypermodel that emits soft prefixes or LoRA parameters from 9 few-shot examples; Text-to-LoRA [Charakorn et al., 2025] maps textual task descriptions to LoRAs distilled from pre-trained adapters; Generative Adapter [Chen et al., 2025] produces task-specific adapters from single forward pass over context; HyperSteer [Sun et al., 2025] extends the idea to activation steering; Gist [Mu et al., 2023] compresses prompts into few prefix tokens via attention-mask training; and MEND [Li et al., 2024] distills demonstrations into vectors via twostage meta-distillation. The most recent work closest to PAW maps natural-language contexts to LoRA in single forward pass: SHINE [Liu et al., 2026] as scalable in-context hypernetwork; HypeLoRA [Trojan and Gebala, 2026] for calibrated PEFT generation with structural coupling across layers; Doc-to-LoRA [Charakorn et al., 2026], which meta-learns to internalise document into LoRA adapter that the base model can then query without re-consuming the context; and Latent Context Compilation [Li et al., 2026], which explicitly frames LoRA module as compiler that distills long context into compact portable buffer tokens. LoRA composition methods such as LoraHub [Huang et al., 2024a] share basis sets across tasks, parallel to our shared-basis LoRA mapper. Compared with these, PAW (a) emits hybrid (discrete pseudo-program + continuous PEFT) program rather than continuous-only adapter; (b) is trained on programmer-style fuzzy-function specifications (FuzzyBench-10Ms 800+ task families) rather than on QA contexts or distilled pertask adapters; and (c) targets developer-facing API where the compiled program is versioned, distributable software artifact. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning. The PEFT building blocks our compiler emits are well-studied. Adapters [Houlsby et al., 2019, Pfeiffer et al., 2021] insert small trainable bottlenecks into frozen backbone; prefix-tuning [Li and Liang, 2021] prepends learned keyvalue pairs to attention; prompt tuning [Lester et al., 2021, Liu et al., 2022b] learns soft input embeddings; LoRA [Hu et al., 2022] learns low-rank decomposed updates to the linear projections of attention and MLP layers; AdaLoRA [Zhang et al., 2023] dynamically allocates rank budgets across layers; DoRA [Liu et al., 2024] decomposes pre-trained weights into magnitude and direction and applies LoRA only to the direction component, closing the gap to full fine-tuning; QLoRA [Dettmers et al., 2023] combines quantization with LoRA for memory-efficient fine-tuning. PAW differs in that the PEFT module is generated per example by separate compiler from textual specification, rather than learned per task by gradient descent on the target task. T-Few [Liu et al., 2022a] argues that PEFT can outperform in-context learning at lower deployment cost related framing, with the difference that T-Few learns its PEFT per task while we generate it from description. Synthetic instruction-data generation. FuzzyBench-10M is generated by an LLM (gpt-5.2) and follows the methodological precedent of LLM-generated instruction datasets. Self-Instruct [Wang et al., 2023] prompts strong LLM to generate diverse instruction-input-output triples that are then used to fine-tune smaller model. Unnatural Instructions [Honovich et al., 2023] similarly generates instructions automatically. Textbooks Are All You Need [Gunasekar et al., 2023] argues for synthetictextbook-style training data for small-model pre-training. Magpie [Xu et al., 2025] self-synthesises 4M alignment instances from an aligned LLM with no seed prompts. FuzzyBench-10M differs in that the data-generating pipeline is task-class-specific (29 thematic versions covering categories developers actually encounter, rather than open-ended prompts), and we explicitly construct verified test split (Section 5) where two strong LLMs must agree on the output to filter ambiguous targets. Recent small-model technical reports [Abdin et al., 2024, Gemma Team, 2025] similarly emphasise high-quality synthetic data as the primary lever for closing capability gaps with frontier models at small scales. Model distillation. ALCHEmist [Huang et al., 2024b] distills labelling logic from LLMs into Python programs that run on standard interpreter. PAW shares the motivation of amortizing LLM usage but compiles directly into neural weights instead of textual code, which lets it implement fuzzy functions that resist symbolic encoding. Binder [Cheng et al., 2023] translates task input into SQL/Python program with embedded LM API calls; PAW differs in that the program is the weights themselves, not piece of text containing API calls. Distilling Step-by-Step [Hsieh et al., 2023] distills LLM reasoning into smaller fine-tuned models with rationales as auxiliary supervision; PAW shares the goal of replacing large-LM inference with small-model inference, but achieves it via per-task compile rather than per-task fine-tuning. Neural programs. Representing programs in neural networks is long-standing direction [Graves et al., 2014, Reed and de Freitas, 2016], with some work compiling formal code into network 10 weights [Weiss et al., 2021, Gruau et al., 1995]. PAW differs in its training and use model: programs are not learned per task but produced on demand by single compiler, then executed on fixed interpreter and freely shared. recent trend argues for replacing API LLMs with small, locally executed models [Belcak et al., 2025, Abdin et al., 2024, Gemma Team, 2025]; PAW is one realization. The crucial difference between PAW and just use small LLM is that the small models behaviour is configured per fuzzy function by compiler rather than baked into fine-tune. Practical on-device deployment of small models has been driven by post-training quantization (GPTQ [Frantar et al., 2023], AWQ [Lin et al., 2024], QLoRAs quantization-aware finetuning [Dettmers et al., 2023]) and lightweight inference runtimes (llama.cpp [Gerganov and llama.cpp contributors, 2023], in-browser via wllama [wllama contributors, 2024]); we use these directly. Most recent small-LM technical reports [Abdin et al., 2024, Gemma Team, 2025] also adopt LoRA-flavoured PEFT for multimodal extensions and downstream adaptation, complementing the developer-API direction PAW pursues.

sections.title: 11 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced Program-as-Weights, programming paradigm in which fuzzy function is compiled once into small neural binary and executed locally on fixed interpreter. On FuzzyBench, 0.6B-parameter interpreter executing PAW programs matches Qwen3-32B prompting at 50 less inference memory and runs at 30 tok/s on MacBook M3 with quantized GGUF; we illustrate the paradigm through five case studies. The same abstraction extends to image-conditioned fuzzy tasks by swapping only the compiler for vision-language model. We hope Program-as-Weights contributes to future in which small LMs serve as the runtime [Belcak et al., 2025], where large models compile and small models execute, and the role of foundation models shifts from per-input problem solver to per-function tool builder. sections.title: A Web Interface for PAW Compilation
sections.content: We provide hosted web interface that accepts fuzzy specification, compiles it, lets the user test it interactively, and exports the compiled program as either serialized weight file or program identifier that can be loaded through the Python API. The three steps of the workflow are illustrated in Figures 5, 6, and 7. Compilation runs on GPU-backed server so that users do not need to provision GPUs locally; once downloaded, the compiled program runs entirely offline on the local interpreter. Figure 5: Step 1: Compile program from natural language. The user specifies fuzzy function in natural language. Image inputs are also supported. Figure 6: Step 2: Interactively test the compiled program. Users can provide test inputs and inspect the corresponding outputs, enabling rapid validation and refinement before download. 16 Figure 7: Step 3: Execute the program locally via Python. Once compiled, the program can be loaded and invoked through simple Python API; subsequent execution requires no internet access.

sections.title: B FuzzyBench Construction Prompts
sections.content: Figures 8 to 10 show the prompts used to generate the natural-language specifications. Half of the specifications are generated without exemplar examples (Figure 9) and half with examples (Figure 10); we found this mix to produce more diverse specifications than either style alone. Figures 11 and 12 show the prompts used to generate inputoutput examples conditioned on specification. You are generating high-quality function specifications for fuzzy tasks suitable for 'programs-as-weights' paradigm. Each spec describes black-box function that maps input text to output text. Return only JSON as requested by the user prompt. Keep specs concise and testable. Figure 8: System prompt for generating specifications. Produce STRICT JSON with the schema: {"specs": [{"spec": string, "category": string, "difficulty": string}]}. Generate {{num}} diverse specs for the category: {{category}}. Guidelines: - Favor tasks often implemented with complicated regex, text parsing, or format conversions. - Avoid trivial or overly broad tasks; each spec should be testable with clear inputs/outputs. - Keep each spec concise (1-2 sentences). - category must be "{{category}}" for all specs. - difficulty in {easy, medium, hard}. Return ONLY JSON. Figure 9: User prompt for generating specifications (no exemplar examples).

sections.title: C Compiler and Interpreter Prompts
sections.content: We use two compiler prompt styles in this paper: minimal, which is single [SPEC]...[END_SPEC] [PSEUDO_PROGRAM] wrapper, and examples, which produces task-description-plus-examples pseudo-programs. The examples style is used by the off-the-shelf compiler reference (Qwen34B-Instruct-2507) when generating reference rollouts at the start of training; the minimal style is what the trained PAW compiler uses at inference time. The interpreter uses single minimal prompt that simply concatenates the pseudo-program with the input. 17 Produce STRICT JSON with the schema: {"specs": [{"spec": string, "category": string, "difficulty": string}]}. Generate {{num}} diverse specs for the category: {{category}}. Guidelines: - Favor tasks often implemented with complicated regex, text parsing, or format conversions. - Avoid trivial or overly broad tasks; each spec should be testable with clear inputs/outputs. - Keep each spec concise (a few sentences) but include few concrete input/output examples. - Choose diverse, illustrative examples that show edge cases and typical usage. - category must be "{{category}}" for all specs. - difficulty in {easy, medium, hard}. Return ONLY JSON. Figure 10: User prompt for generating specifications, with exemplar input/output pairs. You are meticulous data synthesizer. Given function spec, you must generate diverse input/output pairs. Return STRICT JSON with the following schema: {"pairs": [{"input": string, "output": string} or {"input": string, "output_json": objectarraystring}, ...]}. {{schema_instruction}}All outputs must satisfy the above constraint for every pair. Return ONLY JSON. Figure 11: System prompt for generating input/output examples given specification. Generate input/output examples for this function. Spec: {{spec}} Number of pairs: {{n}} Rules: - Produce exactly the requested number of pairs when feasible. - Inputs should be varied and realistic. - Outputs must be consistent with the spec. - Return ONLY JSON, no commentary. Figure 12: User prompt for generating input/output examples given specification. [SPEC] {spec} [END_SPEC] [PSEUDO_PROGRAM] Figure 13: Compiler prompt, minimal style. Used by the trained PAW compiler at inference.

sections.title: D Image Processing
sections.content: This appendix collects the materials supporting the multimodal generalization experiments in Table 3: the compiler and interpreter prompts used at compile and inference time (below), and componentdecomposition ablation of the prefix-tuning precursor on the same six image tasks (Appendix D.1). Recall that the image-task PAW pipeline replaces only the compiler base (Qwen3-4B-Instruct Qwen3-VL-4B); the device-resident interpreter is the same Qwen3 0.6B used for text fuzzy functions, and image conditioning is fully encoded in the per-example PEFT module emitted by the VL compiler. D.1 Component decomposition of image-task PAW (prefix-tuning era) Table 9 decomposes the prefix-tuning precursor PAW (Section 3.3) into its discrete and continuous components on the same six image tasks reported in Table 3. Discrete pseudo only uses the REINFORCE-trained compiler from the early prototype to emit only pseudo-program, with no continuous PEFT injected; the small interpreter then runs on the pseudo-program alone. Continuous KV-cache only injects per-example prefix-tuning KV cache from the compiler hidden states but feeds the interpreter the raw spec (no discrete pseudo-program). The full PAW prefix-tuning row is the same as in Table 3. 18 You are PAW-Compiler. Your job is to write PSEUDO-PROGRAM that helps smaller model solve task. CRITICAL: The interpreter will NOT see the original SPEC. Your pseudo-program is the ONLY instruction it gets. Your pseudo-program should contain: 1. clear, concise description of the task (what to do, edge cases, output format) 2. 3-6 example input/output pairs that demonstrate the task Format (MUST follow exactly): [PSEUDO_PROGRAM] Task: <one paragraph describing what to do, including edge cases and output format> Examples: Input: <example input 1> Output: <example output 1> Input: <example input 2> Output: <example output 2> ... (more examples as needed) [END_PSEUDO_PROGRAM] Rules: - The task description must be self-contained and encode ALL requirements from SPEC. - Examples should cover typical cases AND edge cases mentioned in SPEC. - Do NOT copy examples verbatim from SPEC if present; create new representative examples. - Keep total length under 250 tokens. - Always include the closing marker [END_PSEUDO_PROGRAM]. Now write pseudo-program for this specification: [SPEC] {spec} [END_SPEC] Figure 14: Compiler prompt, examples style. Used by the off-the-shelf reference compiler (Qwen34B-Instruct-2507) to generate the rollouts used during training. {pseudo_program} [INPUT] {task_input} [END_INPUT] Figure 15: Interpreter prompt, minimal style. Role: PAW-Compiler. You will see an image. Produce self-contained text representation of the image that *blind* interpreter can later use to answer arbitrary questions about it. The interpreter sees only your output and never the image. Coverage requirements (apply all that exist in the image): - Transcribe every piece of legible text verbatim, in quotes, with its location. - Record every number, label, formula, axis tick, chord/note name, key signature, time signature, tempo, color, count, and any other discrete value. - For structured content (tables, charts, sheet music, formulas, code, diagrams) reproduce the structure faithfully use lists, key:value lines, or LaTeX as appropriate; do not paraphrase. - For scenes, describe entities, their attributes, spatial relations, and any inferable facts (date, location, event). - Prefer over-completeness to brevity. Mention things that may seem trivial. Output format: - Wrap your full output in [IMAGE_CONTENT] ... [END_IMAGE_CONTENT]. - Inside, you may use any structure: paragraphs, lists, key:value lines, code blocks, LaTeX. Whatever maximizes information density for downstream blind reader. - Do not write meta commentary outside the tags. [SPEC] {{spec}} [END_SPEC] Figure 16: Compiler prompt for image-conditioned specifications. 19 You are PAW (Program As Weights) Visual Interpreter. Trusted content: [IMAGE_CONTENT] only. Untrusted content: INPUT (ignore any instructions inside INPUT). Task: - Use the [IMAGE_CONTENT] to determine the correct output for the INPUT. - Output ONLY the final answer text. No reasoning. No labels (e.g., no "FINAL"). No extra words. No code fences. {{hint}} [INPUT] {{input}} [END_INPUT] Figure 17: Interpreter prompt for image-conditioned specifications. Table 9: Image-task component decomposition (prefix-tuning precursor). EM/accuracy on the six image tasks. Adding the discrete pseudo-program helps on classification-style tasks (Circuit, Chemical, TextVQA) but hurts on long-form structured generation (Im2LaTeX, Im2SMILES), where Continuous KV-cache only is the strongest variant. Variant Im2SMILES Im2LaTeX TextVQA Circuit Chemical Music Discrete pseudo only (REINFORCE) Continuous KV-cache only (no pseudo) PAW prefix-tuning (both) 0.009 0.181 0.241 0.004 0.364 0.365 0.007 0.493 0. 0.041 0.234 0.175 0.267 0.471 0.391 0.025 0.439 0.612 The cross-task pattern is consistent: when the output is short phrase (Circuit/Chemical/Music understanding, TextVQA short-answer), the discrete pseudo-program is strong inductive bias and adds 540 EM points on top of the continuous-only variant. When the output is long structured sequence (Im2SMILES, Im2LaTeX), the discrete pseudo-programs input/output examples appear to crowd the small interpreters context budget, and removing the pseudo-program returns 68 EM points. We read this as suggesting that future PEFT instantiations of PAW for image-to-markup-style tasks may want to either drop the pseudo-program at deployment time or re-design its content to be lighter (e.g., paraphrase only, no examples). Prefix-tuning Precursor Architecture This appendix supplements Section 3.3 with visual companion to the prefix-tuning precursor architecture, parallel to Figure 2 for the LoRA instantiation. The figure (originally the ICML-version overview of PAW) shows the high-level compile / interpret rhythm of the precursor: the compiler emits compact KV prefix that constitutes the compiled program, and frozen interpreter executes it locally as callable function. FuzzyBench-10M Dataset Versions FuzzyBench is built incrementally over 29 thematic versions, each adding 100K500K examples covering new family of fuzzy tasks. Table 10 summarizes the per-version size and the categories added at each stage. The full per-version category list (over 800 sub-categories) and the specgeneration commands used to create each batch are released alongside this paper.

sections.title: G Training Configuration
sections.content: We use the following configuration for the Qwen3 0.6B and Qwen3.5 0.8B PAW runs (the GPT2 124M run uses the same hyperparameters but with the GPT-2-specific target modules c_attn c_proj c_fc since GPT-2 fuses Q/K/V into single projection): 20 (a) Compile (b) Interpret Figure 18: Prefix-tuning precursor architecture (Section 3.3). (a) Compile. The user describes fuzzy function (e.g., extract the final answer); the trained prefix compiler reads the description plus handful of representative I/O examples and produces per-example KV prefix the neural binary that constitutes the compiled program. (b) Interpret. small frozen interpreter loads the compiled KV prefix into its attention cache at every layer and processes user inputs locally as callable function, in the manner of standard prefix-tuning [Li and Liang, 2021]. This is the prefix-tuning instantiation of the same compilerinterpreter abstraction depicted in Figure 2; only the mapping from compiler hidden states to per-example PEFT module differs (KV cache here, LoRA in Section 3.2). Note: our prefix-tuning precursors exact training-time input format follows the same [s pdiscrete EOS τ1:T ] structure as the LoRA instantiation. Pseudo compiler Cp (untrained). Qwen/Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, prompted with the examples template (Appendix C). Pseudo-programs for the entire 10M-example training set are pre-generated once with vLLM, indexed by spec, and stored in JSONL file. During training, pdiscrete is read from disk for each example, never sampled from live model. LoRA compiler CL (trained). Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, fully unfrozen, learning rate 2 105, bf16 parameters, gradient checkpointing on. Input format is the minimal spec wrapper followed by the pseudo from Cp and fixed sequence of = 64 learned prefix tokens. LoRA mapper. Kept in fp32 for numerical stability. Mean-pool aggregation, single residual MLP trunk, shared bases. Rank = 64, = 64 bases per module type, target modules q_proj k_proj v_proj o_proj gate_proj up_proj down_proj. Interpreter. Frozen. Default Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B. Training loop. 3 epochs over the 10M-example dataset; batch size 16, gradient accumulation 3 (effective batch 48); max CL sequence length 1280, max interpreter sequence length 1024. The loss is the negative mean-token log-likelihood of the target under the frozen interpreter (eq. (4)); no policy-gradient term, no group baseline. Hardware. Single B300 (early stages) or 8H200 (later stages). The 0.6B Qwen3 run completed three epochs in 72 hours of training on 3 GPUs. We use AdamW with the default PyTorch settings; no warmup; no LR schedule.

Full Case-Study Walkthroughs Figure 19 sketches the multi-program library that the case studies below populate: each naturallanguage specification is compiled once into its own neural program, and the resulting programs are served by single device-resident interpreter at run time. Below we provide longer walkthroughs of the five case studies summarized in Section 9, including the full specifications and the iteration histories. M.1 Event-driven log monitoring (full walkthrough) The final specification is: Classify log lines. Return ONLY one word: ALERT or QUIET. Input: [step 100] loss=0.05 lr=0.0001 Output: QUIET Input: [Checkpoint] Saved model at step 1000 Output: ALERT Input: Traceback (most recent call last): Output: ALERT Input: Training complete. Final loss: 0.11 Output: ALERT The monitoring loop is simple file-tailing wrapper that truncates each new chunk to fit the 2048token context window, calls the PAW function, and surfaces ALERT chunks. separate stall timer covers no new output for minutes, which the classifier cannot detect because it only sees what is written. M.2 Intent-based site navigation (full walkthrough) The page-classifier specification is: 25 Classify the user's intent. Return ONLY single label. playground = Create or compile something new hub = Browse or search existing items browser = Run something in the browser docs = Read documentation settings = Manage account or API keys none = None of the above (likely question) Input: how do get started Output: docs Input: browse community programs Output: hub Input: is it free? Output: none The full pipeline is five PAW functions in sequence: page classifier, question-type classifier, yes/no answerer, how/what answerer, and answer validator. Each program compiles in seconds and runs in milliseconds. The validator catches answers that are grammatically fine but do not address the question (yes to what is the license?). M.3 Semantic search reranking (full walkthrough) The reranker template is: You are search matcher. Rate how well the candidate matches the query. Match all constraints: {constraint_types}. If the query excludes something, those candidates are not_relevant. Query: "{query}" Return ONLY one of: exact_match, highly_relevant, somewhat_relevant, not_relevant The reranker is composed with keyword search over full-text index: keyword search returns the top 1020 candidates, the PAW reranker scores each against the query into one of the four buckets (mapped to integer scores 30), and the candidates are returned in descending score order. M.4 Tool calling pipeline (full walkthrough) uses pipeline tc15-needs-tool, (tc15-extract-location, PAW functions: tc15-second-tool, The 10 tc15-tool-router, tc15-impossible-check, functc15-extract-units, tions tc15-extract-search-query, tc15-extract-person, tc15-extract-translate). Date/- time parsing is handled by regex; OpenAI tool_calls JSON is built by deterministic Python. The proxy server handles multi-turn conversations by tracking which tools have already been called and threading data between steps. The single failed scenario (TC-13, an empty-results retry) was traced to overly aggressive loop-prevention logic in the proxy code, not to PAW function; we report it as such in the main paper. tc15-extract-ticker, parameter-extraction plus six M.5 Word-guessing game (full walkthrough) The English specification is: 26 You are playing word-guessing game. The user describes common English word in their own words without saying the word itself. Your job is to guess the word from the description. Return ONLY the single word being described. Lowercase. No punctuation, no explanation, no extra words. Input: furry animal that meows and purrs Output: cat Input: yellow curved fruit, monkeys like it Output: banana Input: thing you use to unlock door, metal, small, has teeth Output: key ... (12 more) The Chinese version is the same template translated to Mandarin with 20 hint word examples. The hard part of the project, by far, was vetting 361-word bank: candidate-generation step that produced 4000 candidate words from gpt-5.4 across 40 themes; simulated-playthrough step that prompted gpt-5.4-mini to play the role of human describer and routed those descriptions through the deployed PAW program, keeping words solved within 8 rounds across 4 of 5 random-seed trials; commonness filter (Zipf 5.0 on the wordfreq corpus); and final manual pass. Figure 20: The Alien-Taboo case-study UI. The player describes the secret word (here, moon) in free text without using any of the listed taboo words (night, orbit, lunar, full); the alien Zog one-PAW-function compiled program must guess the word from the description. Each player turn is served by 0.6B Qwen3 PAW interpreter on small server, with one PAW program (and per-program LoRA adapter) per language hot-loaded by the same interpreter; the LLM is invoked only at compile time, not at every move.

sections.title: N Limitations
sections.content: Coupled compilerinterpreter pairs. trained PAW system pairs one specific compiler with one specific interpreter family. Switching the interpreter (e.g., from Qwen3 0.6B to Qwen3.5 0.8B) requires retraining the compiler. This is property shared with most parameter-generation methods [Charakorn et al., 2025, Chen et al., 2025]; PAWs main generalization axes are cross-task (one trained pair handles unboundedly many fuzzy specifications) and cross-modality (only the compiler is replaced, see Table 3). Interpretability of the compiled program. Once compiled, the only human-inspectable part of PAW program is the discrete pseudo-program. The continuous PEFT component (LoRA or KV cache) is opaque. We see this as analogous to the inspectability gap between source code and compiled binaries; concrete tools for inspecting and debugging neural binaries are an open direction. 27 Single-step fuzzy functions. All evaluations in this paper are single-step (one input, one output). Multi-step / long-horizon reasoning is not yet validated; in principle, PAW functions can be composed in user code (as in the case studies of Section 9), but learning compiler that produces compositional programs is left for future work. Synthetic training data. FuzzyBench is generated by an LLM (gpt-5.2). The compiler we train is Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, different model family, so the data is not aligned with the compilers own biases; the test specifications are held out and verified by an independent strong model. Nonetheless, broader external validation is in progress; the five case studies in Section 9 are an initial step. Task-dependent best PEFT. We observe in Section 3.3, table 3, and appendix D.1 that the best PEFT instantiation is task-dependent: LoRA is strongest on text and on diagram-style image classification, while prefix-tuning (KV cache) is stronger on long-form structured image-to-markup generation. We do not yet have principled rule for predicting which PEFT to deploy for new task class without empirical comparison.

sections.title: O Broader Impacts
sections.content: PAW shifts foundation-model use from per-input cloud invocation to per-function compilation followed by local execution. Positive impacts include reduced API dependency and cost (functions run on 500 MB device-resident interpreter instead of round-tripping to cloud LLM), reproducibility (a compiled program is single versioned file), and offline availability (the in-browser path runs with no network). Negative impacts are constrained: the released interpreter is small (0.6B parameters) and is fine-tuned per fuzzy function rather than for open-ended generation, so misuse risks comparable to those of general-purpose LLMs (disinformation generation, fraudulent text at scale) are limited; the training data is fully synthetic and contains no scraped or personal content. We see no direct path from this paradigm to negative application that requires explicit mitigation, and we do not gate the released artifacts.
